summary:
Emperor Wu of Han (reigned 141–87 BC) was the most ambitious ruler of the [[event:77|Han Dynasty]], whose fifty-four-year reign transformed China from a recovering post-Qin state into an expansionist empire that defeated the [[event:1133|Xiongnu]], opened the [[event:1129|Silk Road]], incorporated Vietnam and Korea, and established Confucianism as state ideology — at the cost of severe financial strain and social disruption.

Emperor Wu's foreign policy was characterized by aggressive expansion. He launched repeated campaigns against the [[event:1130|Han-Xiongnu Wars]], eventually breaking the Xiongnu's power and pushing China's frontier far into Central Asia. His sponsorship of [[event:1128|Zhang Qian's Western Missions]] opened diplomatic and commercial contact with Central Asian kingdoms and ultimately enabled [[event:1129|Silk Road]] trade with the Mediterranean world. To finance these wars, he established state monopolies on salt and iron, debased the currency, and imposed new taxes that enriched the treasury but burdened the peasantry. His establishment of Confucian academies and civil service examinations based on Confucian texts in [[event:1131|Confucianism as State Ideology]] laid the institutional foundation for the scholarly-bureaucratic system that would govern China for two millennia.
